title: CML (Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia)
oneLiner: A myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by BCR-ABL fusion; presents with high WCC, splenomegaly; responds dramatically to tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), but untreated can progress to blast crisis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Leukostasis (hyperviscosity syndrome):** WCC > 100 × 10⁹/L with neurological/respiratory symptoms — treat urgently with leukapheresis. *Key finding:* blasts + very high WCC + confusion/dyspnoea/priapism.
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS):** spontaneous or after starting TKIs — monitor K⁺, urate, phosphate, Ca²⁺. *Key finding:* rapidly rising potassium or urate within 24-48h of treatment.
- **Blast crisis (acute transformation):** fever, bone pain, bleeding, blasts >20% in blood or marrow — need immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics and haematology consult. *Key finding:* blasts on blood film + clinical deterioration.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, low-grade fever (B symptoms)
  - Abdominal fullness / left upper quadrant pain (splenomegaly)
  - Asymptomatic — picked up on routine FBC (incidental leukocytosis)
  - Bruising, bleeding (rare in chronic phase)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable splenomegaly (often massive, >15 cm)
  - Hepatomegaly (less common)
  - Pallor (if anaemic), but rarely jaundice
  - Few lymph nodes (lymphadenopathy suggests transformation)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Leukaemoid reaction** (reactive neutrophilia): no basophilia, no splenomegaly, no BCR-ABL, positive infection/stress.
- **Other myeloproliferative neoplasms (PV, ET, MF):** high RBC/Hct in PV, high platelets in ET, splenomegaly + myelofibrosis in MF. *Splitter:* basophilia ± eosinophilia strongly suggests CML.
- **Chronic myelomonocytic leukaemia (CMML):** monocytosis >1 × 10⁹/L, no BCR-ABL.
- **Acute leukaemia (AML/ALL):** blasts >20%, rapid onset, pancytopenia, no chronic phase.
- **Bone marrow fibrosis / metastatic cancer:** imaging and BCR-ABL negative.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Blood film (must look for blasts, basophils, eosinophils)
  - Peripheral blood basophil count (elevated in CML)
  - Urine dip (uric acid if TLS suspected)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + differential (high WCC, left shift, basophilia, may have mild anaemia/normal platelets)
  - U&E, creatinine, LFTs, urate, LDH, calcium, phosphate (TLS screen)
  - Coagulation screen (bleeding risk)
  - Hepatitis B/C, HIV serology (before TKIs)
- **Imaging:**
  - Abdominal ultrasound (confirm splenomegaly, exclude portal hypertension)
  - CXR (baseline, exclude infection before TKIs if neutropenic)
- **Special tests:**
  - **BCR-ABL by RT-PCR** (peripheral blood or bone marrow) — diagnostic gold standard
  - **Cytogenetics (bone marrow):** t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome (Ph+) by karyotype or FISH
  - Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (confirm phase, assess fibrosis, blast count)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Presence of BCR-ABL fusion by PCR/FISH (or t(9;22) on karyotype)
  - No WHO-defined blast phase (blasts <10% chronic phase, 10-19% accelerated, ≥20% blast phase)
- **Never-miss test:** Blood film + urgent haematology review (do not wait for PCR if film suspicious).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If WCC > 100 × 10⁹/L with symptoms → discuss leukapheresis
  - If TLS risk (high tumour burden, high urate) → IV fluids + allopurinol (or rasburicase if urate high) [check local policy]
  - If febrile / suspected infection → blood cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics
  - IV fluids 2-3 L/day if TLS risk
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start TKI therapy once BCR-ABL confirmed (or strongly suspected with high WCC + basophilia)
  - Hydroxyurea 1-3 g/day PO for rapid cytoreduction if WCC > 50-100 (until TKI takes effect)
  - Monitor FBC daily (watch for rapid fall → neutropenia, thrombocytopenia)
  - Allopurinol 300 mg daily PO for TLS prophylaxis
  - Tumour lysis monitoring: U&E, urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH every 6-12h initially
  - Baseline ECG (QTc interval for dasatinib/nilotinib), cardiac history (TKI-specific side effects)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - TKI of choice: imatinib 400 mg daily (first-line), dasatinib 100 mg daily, nilotinib 400 mg BD
  - Monitor molecular response by BCR-ABL PCR every 3 months
  - Manage TKI side effects: fluid retention (imatinib), pleural effusion (dasatinib), metabolic (nilotinib)
  - Annual cardiovascular risk assessment (nilotinib/dasatinib)
  - If failure or intolerance → switch TKI, consider allogenic transplant
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1 L/8h (adjust for TLS risk)
  - VTE prophylaxis: if not bleeding, standard LMWH
  - Diet: NBM if neutrophil count < 0.5 (for oral care) — but usual diet acceptable
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly, escalate as per local criteria
  - Daily FBC, U&E, urate, LDH for first 3 days
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Imatinib (Glivec):** 400 mg PO once daily with food (increase to 600-800 mg if failure to respond) [check local policy]
  - **Dasatinib (Sprycel):** 100 mg PO once daily (or 140 mg for accelerated phase) [check local policy]
  - **Nilotinib (Tasigna):** 400 mg PO twice daily on empty stomach (do not eat 2h before/after) [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Bosutinib, ponatinib (for resistant T315I mutation) — specialist only
  - Hydroxyurea 500 mg - 3 g daily PO (for cytoreduction before TKI or in acute setting)
- **Key interactions:**
  - Imatinib inhibits CYP3A4 → avoid grapefruit, St John's wort
  - Dasatinib and nilotinib prolong QTc → avoid drugs that prolong QTc (e.g. macrolides, antipsychotics)
  - TKIs may increase statin levels → monitor
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Imatinib: no dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment. Hepatic: reduce dose if bilirubin >3x ULN. [check local policy]
  - Dasatinib: no adjustment for renal, but avoid if CrCl <30 (limited data). Hepatic: reduce if moderate-severe.
  - Nilotinib: caution in hepatic impairment — reduce dose if Child-Pugh B/C. QTc dependent.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting dasatinib or nilotinib without checking baseline QTc (risk of torsades) — get ECG before first dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (first days of treatment)
  - Neutropenia/ thrombocytopenia (from TKI — especially imatinib, first 2-4 weeks)
  - Pleural effusion (dasatinib) — check respiratory exam, CXR
  - Fluid retention (imatinib) — peripheral oedema, rarely pericardial/pleural
- **Chronic:**
  - Blast crisis (transformation to AML/ALL) — median 3-5 years if no TKI, rare on TKI
  - Accelerated phase (drug resistance, clonal evolution)
  - Cardiovascular events (nilotinib: arterial thrombosis, dasatinib: pulmonary hypertension)
  - Immunosuppression (TKIs can cause lymphopenia)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent with TKI: >90% overall survival at 10 years if chronic phase and compliant. Deep molecular response (MR4.5) achievable. Without treatment, median survival 3-5 years. Transformation to blast crisis is fatal if not salvageable.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do send urgent blood film + call haematology registrar** if WCC > 50 with basophilia/splenomegaly — do not wait for confirmatory PCR to start hydroxyurea.
- **Always check urate + start allopurinol** before starting TKI to prevent TLS, especially if WCC > 100 or LDH high.
- **Monitor BCR-ABL PCR every 3 months** — failure to achieve a major molecular response by 12 months signals resistance (consider mutation testing).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start TKI without confirmatory BCR-ABL test** (unless overwhelming suspicion and haematology agrees) — misdiagnosis (e.g. leukaemoid reaction) leads to unnecessary toxicity.
- **Do not ignore progressive anaemia/thrombocytopenia** on TKI — may indicate drug failure, bone marrow fibrosis, or transformation.
- **Do not forget pregnancy test in any woman of childbearing age** — TKIs are teratogenic; require effective contraception.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New onset shortness of breath (pleural effusion), chest pain (TKI cardiac effects), fever/rigors (neutropenia), easy bruising/bleeding.
- **Return if:** Symptoms of hyperviscosity (headache, blurred vision, confusion) — especially if WCC not yet normalised.
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 1-2 weeks for TKI initiation review; then FBC weekly until stable; BCR-ABL PCR at 3, 6, 12 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or clinical concern → call ST4+ haematology registrar.
- **Blasts in blood (any) with fever/rigors** → treat as neutropenic sepsis + call haematology/oncology.
- **Rapidly rising K⁺ or urate despite fluids + allopurinol** → consider haemodialysis if TLS refractory, call ITU.
- **Suspected transfusion reaction** → stop TKI? Discuss with haematology; manage per local transfusion policy.

rawMarkdown:
## CML (Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia)
**One-liner:** A myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by BCR-ABL fusion; presents with high WCC, splenomegaly; responds dramatically to tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), but untreated can progress to blast crisis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Leukostasis (hyperviscosity syndrome):** WCC > 100 × 10⁹/L with neurological/respiratory symptoms — treat urgently with leukapheresis. *Key finding:* blasts + very high WCC + confusion/dyspnoea/priapism.
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS):** spontaneous or after starting TKIs — monitor K⁺, urate, phosphate, Ca²⁺. *Key finding:* rapidly rising potassium or urate within 24-48h of treatment.
- **Blast crisis (acute transformation):** fever, bone pain, bleeding, blasts >20% in blood or marrow — need immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics and haematology consult. *Key finding:* blasts on blood film + clinical deterioration.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, low-grade fever (B symptoms)
  - Abdominal fullness / left upper quadrant pain (splenomegaly)
  - Asymptomatic — picked up on routine FBC (incidental leukocytosis)
  - Bruising, bleeding (rare in chronic phase)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable splenomegaly (often massive, >15 cm)
  - Hepatomegaly (less common)
  - Pallor (if anaemic), but rarely jaundice
  - Few lymph nodes (lymphadenopathy suggests transformation)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Leukaemoid reaction** (reactive neutrophilia): no basophilia, no splenomegaly, no BCR-ABL, positive infection/stress.
- **Other myeloproliferative neoplasms (PV, ET, MF):** high RBC/Hct in PV, high platelets in ET, splenomegaly + myelofibrosis in MF. *Splitter:* basophilia ± eosinophilia strongly suggests CML.
- **Chronic myelomonocytic leukaemia (CMML):** monocytosis >1 × 10⁹/L, no BCR-ABL.
- **Acute leukaemia (AML/ALL):** blasts >20%, rapid onset, pancytopenia, no chronic phase.
- **Bone marrow fibrosis / metastatic cancer:** imaging and BCR-ABL negative.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Blood film (must look for blasts, basophils, eosinophils)
  - Peripheral blood basophil count (elevated in CML)
  - Urine dip (uric acid if TLS suspected)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC + differential (high WCC, left shift, basophilia, may have mild anaemia/normal platelets)
  - U&E, creatinine, LFTs, urate, LDH, calcium, phosphate (TLS screen)
  - Coagulation screen (bleeding risk)
  - Hepatitis B/C, HIV serology (before TKIs)
- **Imaging:**
  - Abdominal ultrasound (confirm splenomegaly, exclude portal hypertension)
  - CXR (baseline, exclude infection before TKIs if neutropenic)
- **Special tests:**
  - **BCR-ABL by RT-PCR** (peripheral blood or bone marrow) — diagnostic gold standard
  - **Cytogenetics (bone marrow):** t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome (Ph+) by karyotype or FISH
  - Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (confirm phase, assess fibrosis, blast count)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Presence of BCR-ABL fusion by PCR/FISH (or t(9;22) on karyotype)
  - No WHO-defined blast phase (blasts <10% chronic phase, 10-19% accelerated, ≥20% blast phase)
- **Never-miss test:** Blood film + urgent haematology review (do not wait for PCR if film suspicious).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If WCC > 100 × 10⁹/L with symptoms → discuss leukapheresis
  - If TLS risk (high tumour burden, high urate) → IV fluids + allopurinol (or rasburicase if urate high) [check local policy]
  - If febrile / suspected infection → blood cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics
  - IV fluids 2-3 L/day if TLS risk
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start TKI therapy once BCR-ABL confirmed (or strongly suspected with high WCC + basophilia)
  - Hydroxyurea 1-3 g/day PO for rapid cytoreduction if WCC > 50-100 (until TKI takes effect)
  - Monitor FBC daily (watch for rapid fall → neutropenia, thrombocytopenia)
  - Allopurinol 300 mg daily PO for TLS prophylaxis
  - Tumour lysis monitoring: U&E, urate, phosphate, calcium, LDH every 6-12h initially
  - Baseline ECG (QTc interval for dasatinib/nilotinib), cardiac history (TKI-specific side effects)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - TKI of choice: imatinib 400 mg daily (first-line), dasatinib 100 mg daily, nilotinib 400 mg BD
  - Monitor molecular response by BCR-ABL PCR every 3 months
  - Manage TKI side effects: fluid retention (imatinib), pleural effusion (dasatinib), metabolic (nilotinib)
  - Annual cardiovascular risk assessment (nilotinib/dasatinib)
  - If failure or intolerance → switch TKI, consider allogenic transplant
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl 1 L/8h (adjust for TLS risk)
  - VTE prophylaxis: if not bleeding, standard LMWH
  - Diet: NBM if neutrophil count < 0.5 (for oral care) — but usual diet acceptable
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-6 hourly, escalate as per local criteria
  - Daily FBC, U&E, urate, LDH for first 3 days

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Imatinib (Glivec):** 400 mg PO once daily with food (increase to 600-800 mg if failure to respond) [check local policy]
  - **Dasatinib (Sprycel):** 100 mg PO once daily (or 140 mg for accelerated phase) [check local policy]
  - **Nilotinib (Tasigna):** 400 mg PO twice daily on empty stomach (do not eat 2h before/after) [check local policy]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Bosutinib, ponatinib (for resistant T315I mutation) — specialist only
  - Hydroxyurea 500 mg - 3 g daily PO (for cytoreduction before TKI or in acute setting)
- **Key interactions:**
  - Imatinib inhibits CYP3A4 → avoid grapefruit, St John's wort
  - Dasatinib and nilotinib prolong QTc → avoid drugs that prolong QTc (e.g. macrolides, antipsychotics)
  - TKIs may increase statin levels → monitor
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Imatinib: no dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal impairment. Hepatic: reduce dose if bilirubin >3x ULN. [check local policy]
  - Dasatinib: no adjustment for renal, but avoid if CrCl <30 (limited data). Hepatic: reduce if moderate-severe.
  - Nilotinib: caution in hepatic impairment — reduce dose if Child-Pugh B/C. QTc dependent.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting dasatinib or nilotinib without checking baseline QTc (risk of torsades) — get ECG before first dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (first days of treatment)
  - Neutropenia/ thrombocytopenia (from TKI — especially imatinib, first 2-4 weeks)
  - Pleural effusion (dasatinib) — check respiratory exam, CXR
  - Fluid retention (imatinib) — peripheral oedema, rarely pericardial/pleural
- **Chronic:**
  - Blast crisis (transformation to AML/ALL) — median 3-5 years if no TKI, rare on TKI
  - Accelerated phase (drug resistance, clonal evolution)
  - Cardiovascular events (nilotinib: arterial thrombosis, dasatinib: pulmonary hypertension)
  - Immunosuppression (TKIs can cause lymphopenia)

### Prognosis
- Excellent with TKI: >90% overall survival at 10 years if chronic phase and compliant. Deep molecular response (MR4.5) achievable. Without treatment, median survival 3-5 years. Transformation to blast crisis is fatal if not salvageable.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do send urgent blood film + call haematology registrar** if WCC > 50 with basophilia/splenomegaly — do not wait for confirmatory PCR to start hydroxyurea.
- **Always check urate + start allopurinol** before starting TKI to prevent TLS, especially if WCC > 100 or LDH high.
- **Monitor BCR-ABL PCR every 3 months** — failure to achieve a major molecular response by 12 months signals resistance (consider mutation testing).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start TKI without confirmatory BCR-ABL test** (unless overwhelming suspicion and haematology agrees) — misdiagnosis (e.g. leukaemoid reaction) leads to unnecessary toxicity.
- **Do not ignore progressive anaemia/thrombocytopenia** on TKI — may indicate drug failure, bone marrow fibrosis, or transformation.
- **Do not forget pregnancy test in any woman of childbearing age** — TKIs are teratogenic; require effective contraception.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New onset shortness of breath (pleural effusion), chest pain (TKI cardiac effects), fever/rigors (neutropenia), easy bruising/bleeding.
- **Return if:** Symptoms of hyperviscosity (headache, blurred vision, confusion) — especially if WCC not yet normalised.
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 1-2 weeks for TKI initiation review; then FBC weekly until stable; BCR-ABL PCR at 3, 6, 12 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or clinical concern → call ST4+ haematology registrar.
- **Blasts in blood (any) with fever/rigors** → treat as neutropenic sepsis + call haematology/oncology.
- **Rapidly rising K⁺ or urate despite fluids + allopurinol** → consider haemodialysis if TLS refractory, call ITU.
- **Suspected transfusion reaction** → stop TKI? Discuss with haematology; manage per local transfusion policy.
